title: Agentic Workflows
lesson:
An AI agent is a model that can take actions in a loop until a job is done. An agentic workflow is the shape of that work — the route the task travels, the steps it passes through, and the rules deciding what happens next. If the agent is the worker, the workflow is the floor plan of the workshop: where things start, which stations they visit, and where they exit. This topic is about designing that floor plan well, because a clever model on a messy route still produces mush.

The kitchen analogy

Think of a busy restaurant kitchen. A single cook making one dish start to finish is a plain prompt — one in, one out. An agentic workflow is the whole line: an order comes in, goes to prep, then the grill, then plating, and a head chef checks it before it leaves.

Each station does one focused job and hands the plate on. Some steps must happen in order (you can't plate before you cook); others can run side by side (someone chops veg while the sauce simmers). Designing a workflow is deciding those stations, their order, and who inspects the result.

Workflows vs free-roaming agents

There's a useful split, popularised by Anthropic in 2024 and still the standard framing in 2026:

  • A workflow is where you, the builder, lay out the steps in code ahead of time — the model fills in each step but the path is fixed.
  • A pure agent decides its own path at runtime, choosing tools and steps freely.

Workflows are predictable and cheap to debug; free agents are flexible but harder to control.

Orchestrated workflowAutonomous agent
You fix the steps in codeModel picks steps at runtime
Predictable, easy to traceFlexible, can surprise you
Best for known, repeatable jobsBest for open-ended tasks
Cheaper, faster, fewer model callsMore calls, more cost, more risk

The grown-up advice: start with the simplest thing that works. Most real products are workflows with a sprinkle of agency, not fully autonomous robots.

Common workflow patterns

A few shapes come up again and again. Learn these and you can build most things:

  • Prompt chaining: step 1's output becomes step 2's input — outline an article, write it, then proofread it. Like an assembly line.
  • Routing: a first model reads the request and sends it down the right branch — refunds one way, technical issues another. Like a triage nurse.
  • Parallelisation: fan the work out to several model calls at once, then combine — three reviewers grade an essay and you average them.
  • Orchestrator–worker: a lead model breaks a big task into pieces, hands each to a worker, and stitches results back — close to Multi-Agent Systems.
  • Evaluator–optimiser: one model drafts, a second critiques, the first revises — a loop that repeats until the critique is satisfied.

That last pattern is the head-chef-checks-the-plate idea, and it's one of the biggest quality wins available. Here is a tiny version in code:

draft = llm("Write a 2-line product blurb: " + brief)
for _ in range(3):                      # cap the loop!
    review = llm(f"Critique this blurb. Reply DONE if great.\n{draft}")
    if "DONE" in review:
        break
    draft = llm(f"Rewrite using this feedback:\n{review}\n\n{draft}")
return draft

Notice the range(3). The loop is capped. An agentic workflow without a stopping rule can spin forever, burning money and time — always give it a budget of steps.

What an agent does inside a step

Each agentic step usually runs a small loop: the model thinks, it picks an action (often via Tool Calling or Function Calling — its way of asking your code to run something), it sees the result, and decides what to do next.

That observe-think-act cycle is the engine; the workflow is the track it runs on. Reasoning-heavy jobs lean on Reasoning Models for the thinking part, and many steps pull fresh facts through RAG.

Why this matters in real products

Coding assistants that read a repo, edit files, run tests, and fix what broke are agentic workflows. So are research assistants that search, read, and summarise; support flows that classify, look up an order, and draft a reply; and data pipelines that clean, transform, and check.

The model is the smart bit, but reliability comes from the structure around it — clear steps, capped loops, and checkpoints. Good prompts and tight system prompts at each station keep every worker on task. Wiring it all together is the craft of AI Orchestration.

💡 Rule of thumb: add autonomy only when a fixed path can't cope. Every bit of freedom you hand the model is freedom to surprise you — sometimes brilliantly, sometimes expensively.
takeaways: The workflow is the route; the agent is the worker on it.
Start with fixed steps; add autonomy only when a path can't cope.
Always cap loops, validate between steps, and add a review checkpoint.

diagram.caption: Three core shapes — chaining (top), routing (middle), and an evaluator loop (right) — all wrapped by the golden rule: cap the loops and add a checkpoint.

quiz.q: What is an agentic workflow, as opposed to the agent itself?
options: The pre-designed structure of steps and rules the task travels through
The specific model weights the agent runs on
The single prompt you send to the model
The GPU that serves the model
quiz.explain: The agent is the worker; the workflow is the floor plan — the steps a task moves through and the logic deciding what comes next. It is structure, not the model or hardware.

quiz.q: You're building a bot that always does the same three steps in a known order. Which approach fits best?
options: A fully autonomous agent that picks its own steps each time
An orchestrated workflow with the steps fixed in code
One giant prompt that does all three steps at once
A multi-agent swarm with no coordinator
quiz.explain: For known, repeatable jobs a fixed workflow is predictable, cheaper, and easy to trace. Autonomy only earns its keep when a fixed path genuinely can't cope.

quiz.q: A support flow first labels a message as 'refund' or 'technical', then sends it down a matching branch. Which pattern is that?
quiz.explain: Routing uses a first model to classify the request and send it down the right branch, like a triage nurse. Chaining feeds outputs forward; parallelisation runs calls at once.

quiz.q: In the evaluator–optimiser code, why is the loop wrapped in range(3) instead of running until the critic is happy?
options: Three rewrites always produce perfect text
It caps the loop so it can't spin forever and burn cost and time
Python loops require a number to run
It makes the model reason more carefully
quiz.explain: An agentic loop with no stopping rule can run endlessly, racking up cost and latency. The cap gives it a step budget and a guaranteed exit even if the critic is never satisfied.

mentalModel: An agent is the worker; the agentic workflow is the kitchen floor plan it works in — the stations, their order, who can run in parallel, and who inspects the plate before it leaves. Design the route, cap the loops, add checkpoints.
mistakes: Reaching for a fully autonomous agent first. A free-roaming model that picks its own steps is the hardest thing to debug and the easiest to send off the rails. Start with a fixed workflow and add freedom only where a set path genuinely can't cope.

No stopping rule. An evaluator–optimiser or tool loop with no step cap can spin forever, racking up cost and latency. Always set a maximum number of iterations and a clear 'good enough' exit, like the range(3) in the example.

One giant prompt doing everything. Cramming classify, look-up, reason, and write into a single call makes failures impossible to locate. Split the job into focused stations (chaining or routing) so you can see exactly which step broke.

Forgetting that errors compound. If each of five steps is 90% reliable, the whole chain is only about 0.9⁵ ≈ 59% reliable. Add validation between steps and don't blindly feed a bad output into the next stage.
Letting context pile up unchecked. Each step's output stacks into the prompt, and long agent runs can blow past the context window or just bury the important bits. Summarise or trim between steps.

Skipping the checkpoint step. The biggest cheap win — a second model (or a simple rule) reviewing the output before it ships — is the one people leave out. A draft-then-critique loop catches most embarrassing mistakes.
exercise:
Design (on paper or in code) a two-stage workflow for a support bot. Stage 1 — Routing: a model reads an incoming message and labels it as refund, technical, or other. Stage 2 — Handling: based on the label, send it down a different prompt with a different system prompt (e.g. the refund path is polite and policy-bound; the technical path asks clarifying questions). Run five made-up messages through it and check each lands on the right branch and gets a sensibly different reply.

Stretch goal: Add a third evaluator–optimiser stage: a reviewer model reads the drafted reply, and if it spots a problem (rude tone, missing info), it sends feedback back to the handler for one rewrite. Cap it at two rewrite attempts so it can't loop forever.
solution: The key insight is that separating routing from handling makes the whole thing both better and easier to debug. When a reply is wrong, you can immediately see whether the router mislabelled it (stage 1) or the handler wrote a poor answer (stage 2) — you couldn't tell those apart in one mega-prompt. For the router, ask the model to reply with only the label word so your code can branch on it cleanly; validate that it returned one of the three allowed labels and fall back to other if not. For the stretch goal, the win to watch for is the reviewer catching a tone or completeness issue and the rewrite fixing it — and crucially, the loop stopping after two tries even when the reviewer is never fully satisfied. That cap is what separates a robust workflow from a runaway one.
summary: An agentic workflow is the structure around an AI agent — the steps a task travels through and the rules choosing what happens next. The core choice is orchestrated workflows (you fix the steps in code: predictable and cheap) versus autonomous agents (the model picks its own path: flexible but risky); start simple and add autonomy only when needed. Lean on proven patterns — prompt chaining, routing, parallelisation, orchestrator–worker, and evaluator–optimiser. Always cap your loops, validate between steps, and add a review checkpoint, because reliability comes from the structure, not just the model.

keyTerms.def: The pre-designed structure around an AI agent: the steps a task moves through and the logic deciding what comes next.
A flow where the builder fixes the steps in code ahead of time; the model fills in each step but the path is set.
A model that decides its own steps and tool choices at runtime — flexible but harder to predict and debug.
Feeding one step's output into the next, like an assembly line (e.g. outline, then draft, then proofread).
Using a first model to classify a request and send it down the matching branch, like a triage nurse.
Running several model calls at once on parts of a task, then combining the results.
A lead model splits a big task into pieces, hands each to a worker, and stitches the results back together.
A loop where one model drafts, a second critiques, and the first revises — repeating until the critique passes.
A hard limit on how many times a loop may run, so an agentic workflow can't spin forever and burn cost.
A review step — by a model or a simple rule — that inspects an output before it moves on or ships.
